w:r.w:t: Wedify: Wedding Planner and Venue Comparison Web Application
This project is dedicated to our parents, teachers, and mentors for their guidance and support.
We gratefully acknowledge our supervisor, Sir Daniyal Adeeb, for his valuable mentorship.
Wedify: Wedding Planner and Venue Comparison Web Application
We thank Allah Almighty for granting us the strength to complete this final year project.
We sincerely thank our respected supervisor, Sir Daniyal Adeeb, for continuous guidance and support.
We also thank the Department of Software Engineering, UMT, for providing an enabling learning environment.
Finally, we are grateful to our families and classmates for their encouragement throughout the project.
Objective: Centralized platform for venue discovery, budget estimation, vendor browsing, and wedding planning in Lahore
Undertaken by: Sherana Farooq (S2023332005), Saifullah (S2023332009), Hafiz Inam (S2023332026)
Tools Used: Next.js, React, Tailwind CSS, JavaScript, Lucide React, GitHub, VS Code
Documentation: Final Report, Backlog, Architecture Diagrams, Traceability Matrix, Test Evidence
Turnitin similarity report will be attached in final submission.
Pledge: We declare this report and implementation are our own original academic work.
All references and sources have been properly acknowledged where applicable.
Student Signatures: Sherana Farooq | Saifullah | Hafiz Inam

I have carefully examined the documentation of the Final Year Project titled 'Wedify: Wedding Planner and Venue Comparison Web Application'; and I endorse that this documentation complies with undergraduate-level FYP report standards.
The document has been checked for plagiarism through Turnitin software available in UMT Library. The similarities of the document are within acceptable range.
Moreover, the accompanying CDs contain PDF of the documentation, as well as the source code and binaries with user manual and installation guide.
FYP Supervisor Name: Sir Daniyal Adeeb
Wedify is a web-based platform that helps users discover and compare wedding venues in Lahore.
The system provides budget calculation, vendor discovery, favorites management, and wedding checklist tracking.
It is developed using Next.js, React, and Tailwind CSS with a component-based and maintainable architecture.
The project improves planning efficiency, budget transparency, and decision-making for wedding clients.

This chart contains a history of this documentâ€™s revisions. The entries below are provided solely for illustration purposes. Those entries should be deleted until the revision/s they refer to have actually been created.

The document itself should be stored in revision control, and a brief description of each version should be entered in the Revision Control System. A brief description can be repeated in this section. Revisions need not be described elsewhere in the document, unless they explain the document.
Initial draft created for distribution and review comments
Second draft incorporating initial review comments, distributed for final review
First complete draft, which is placed under change control
Revised draft, revised according to the change control process and maintained under change control
Revised draft, revised according to the change control process and maintained under change control

Introduce the domain or field of the project.
Explain the general area of study and how the project fits into it.
Provide a brief history or evolution of related technologies or
Contoso Pharmaceuticals is a research-intensive company engaged in the development of new drugs. The company employs multiple chemists and pharmacists who work across various laboratorie
are involved in research and development of new drugs

a wide range of chemicals for research and experimentation. However, there is no centralized system in place to track the availability and usage of chemicals across the organization. This leads to inefficient procurement practices, duplication of chemical purchases, and non-compliance risks with government safety regulations concerning sensitive substances.

Theis no track of chemical in the company. One chemical is ordered and received by one chemist or Pharmacist. Container is usually not used entirely but other people donâ€™t know the availability of that chemical in the company. Therefore, whenever someone else needed that same chemical, they again order the same chemical. This result in his financial burden on the company. In addition to this problem, there is another issue of government health and safety department reporting. There are some sensitive dual usage chemicals, for example chemical named
is used in drugs to treat
but it can also be used to make heroine, an infamous illegal narcotic. Government also
Pharmaceutical companies to submit the report of usage of sensitive chemicals in the company in order to avoid misuse of these sensitive chemicals.
There is a need to develop a Chemical tracking system.
â€œThe system should keep track of all the chemical containers
already have in the stockroom and in laboratories.

way, the chemists can get some chemicals from someone down the hall instead of always buying a new container. This should save us a lot of money. Also, the Health and Safety Department needs to generate government reports on chemical usage and disposal with a lot less work than it takes them today.
to buy chemicals from Chemists or Pharmacists

for fulfillment, the vendors will send chemical containers and invoices to Contoso Pharmaceuticals, and Contosoâ€™s purchasing department will pay the vendors. However, those processes take place outside the scope of the Chemical Tracking System, as part of the operations of the purchasing and receiving departments.
t clear that this system is not directly involved in placing orders with the vendors, receiving the products, or paying the bills.

The absence of an inventory tracking mechanism often results in multiple purchases of the same chemical, even when partially used containers are already available within the premises. This practice incurs unnecessary financial costs. Additionally, due to the presence of dual-use chemicals such as ephedrineâ€”which is used in both legitimate pharmaceutical products and illegal narcoticsâ€”there is a pressing need to comply with government reporting requirements regarding the use and disposal of such substances.

A Chemical Tracking System is essential for addressing these challenges. The proposed system will maintain a real-time record of chemical inventory in stockrooms and labs, improve internal visibility, reduce redundant purchases, and facilitate easy generation of compliance reports.
Introduce the domain or field of the project.
Explain the general area of study and how the project fits into it.
Provide a brief history or evolution of related technologies or issues.

Currently, there is no digital system to monitor or track chemical inventory within Contoso Pharmaceuticals. Chemists and pharmacists often unknowingly reorder chemicals that are already available in another lab or storage area. This results in inefficient use of resources and unnecessary financial expenditures. Furthermore, manual processes for regulatory reporting on sensitive chemicals are time-consuming and error-prone, exposing the company to compliance risks.
List the main goals the project aims to achieve.
Include both primary and secondary objectives if applicable.
The primary objectives of this project are:
- To develop a centralized Chemical Tracking System for recording chemical container availability across all laboratories and storage facilities.
- To enable real-time visibility of chemical inventory to all authorized personnel.
- To minimize redundant chemical purchases through better resource utilization.
- To facilitate automated report generation for government regulatory bodies regarding the usage and disposal of sensitive chemicals.
Define the boundaries of the project.
Explain what will be included and what is beyond the scope (limitations).
- Development of a web-based system to record and view the status of chemical containers.
- User roles for chemists, lab managers, and administrators.
- Logging of usage and disposal of sensitive chemicals.
- Report generation module for regulatory compliance.
- Payment processing and vendor communication (done manually by the purchasing department).
Explain the importance and expected impact of the project.
Discuss how it contributes to academia, industry, or society.

This system will significantly reduce the financial burden on Contoso Pharmaceuticals by preventing duplicate chemical purchases. It will also enhance collaboration among research personnel through improved visibility into chemical availability. Most importantly, the system will streamline the regulatory reporting process, helping the company avoid penalties and meet compliance obligations related to hazardous and dual-use chemicals.
describe the scenarios where AI can be used in the project.
To further enhance the functionality and value of the Chemical Tracking System, Artificial Intelligence (AI) can be integrated into the system.
The following are three key AI-based
that are relevant and beneficial for the proposed project:

Predictive Inventory Management: Using machine learning algorithms, the system can analyze historical usage data of chemicals to forecast future demand. This allows the company to: - Proactively manage inventory and reduce chemical waste. - Automatically suggest optimal reorder points for critical chemicals. - Prevent stockouts or overstocking of commonly used substances.

Anomaly Detection for Misuse of Sensitive Chemicals: An AI-powered anomaly detection module can monitor the usage patterns of dual-use or sensitive chemicals (e.g., ephedrine). It can: - Detect unusual activity such as excessive withdrawals or abnormal usage frequency. - Flag potential misuse or unauthorized access to restricted chemicals. - Generate real-time alerts to safety officers and compliance teams.

Automated Regulatory Report Generation: Natural Language Processing (NLP) and data extraction techniques can be used to: - Automatically generate required government compliance reports in standardized formats. - Summarize chemical usage logs in human-readable formats. - Reduce manual workload for the Health and Safety Department, ensuring timely and accurate reporting.
List the expected outputs (e.g., software application, report, hardware prototype).
- A functional web-based Chemical Tracking System.
A brief description of the client with whom you are working
ou will fill this section only if you have a client (contracted
It is a pharmaceutical company with skills and team in new drug discovery and development.
y have a highly skilled team of
chemists and Pharmacist working in different labs that are involved in research and development of new drugs. They extensively use different chemicals for research and experimentation.
List of all stakeholders along with their roles in making of the system
Use chemicals for research and experimentation in laboratories.
Record the usage, quantity, and disposal of chemicals through the system.
Check inventory availability before placing new requests.
Request chemicals from the stockroom or other labs.
Ensure proper labeling and safety compliance during handling.
Receive chemical requests from chemists and pharmacists.
Place purchase orders with external vendors (this is outside the system scope but influenced by it).
Coordinate delivery and invoice processing with vendors.
Update the system manually after receiving new chemical containers.
Manage the physical storage and distribution of chemicals.
Update the system with details of received, available, and dispensed chemicals.
Fulfill chemical requests raised by chemists or pharmacists.
Ensure chemicals are stored according to safety regulations.
Monitor and enforce safety standards for handling hazardous chemicals.
Verify that staff members are trained and certified to use specific hazardous or controlled substances.
Audit chemical usage logs to ensure compliance with internal and external regulations.
Generate and submit periodic reports to government authorities regarding the use, storage, and disposal of sensitive or controlled chemicals (e.g., ephedrine).
Maintain user accounts, roles, and system access controls.
Ensure data integrity, backup, and security of the chemical tracking system.
Provide technical support and maintain system uptime.
Not direct users of the system but receive reports generated by the Health and Safety Department.
Enforce compliance regulations regarding controlled substances.
Review chemical usage data for auditing and legal compliance purposes.
Those impacted by the deployment of the system. This can be a simple list as well as a bulleted one with short
There will be reduced paper work for them.
they will be able to provide better customer care as system will help them respond to queries quickly.
This may include users as well as support groups
Economic Impact: The company will benefit from reduced waste, fewer redundant purchases, and more efficient resource utilization, leading to significant cost savings.
Social Impact: Improved operational efficiency enhances the companyâ€™s reputation as a responsible and compliant pharmaceutical company.
Economic Impact: Faster access to required chemicals improves productivity and shortens drug development cycles, indirectly improving revenue potential.
Social Impact: Scientists work in a safer, more accountable environment with better collaboration among labs.
Economic Impact: Reduced time and effort in compliance reporting and manual inspections, allowing the department to allocate resources more effectively.
Social Impact: Improved workplace safety and lower risk of accidents, chemical exposure, or misuse of hazardous substances.
Social Impact: Improved regulatory compliance and transparency help government agencies monitor and control the usage of dual-use or hazardous chemicals, protecting public health and security.
Economic Impact: Reduced costs in enforcement and auditing due to better self-regulation by companies like Contoso.
Social Impact: Enhanced chemical safety protocols reduce the risk of leaks, spills, or misuse, improving environmental health and community safety.
Economic Impact: Preventing fines or shutdowns due to non-compliance helps maintain local employment and economic activity.
Economic Impact: Improved demand forecasting and order efficiency may reduce overstocking and streamline logistics for suppliers.
Social Impact: Strengthened business relationships with a compliant and efficient client.
Systems and/ or products, this project depends upon for its completion.
Purpose: Manages procurement, finance, inventory, and human resources.
Interaction: Purchase orders and invoices are processed here; chemical tracking data (like low inventory alerts) may influence procurement decisions.
Purpose: Used by the purchasing department to place chemical orders with suppliers.
Interaction: The chemical tracking system may inform purchasing decisions, but the actual order and fulfillment process takes place in external vendor systems.
Purpose: Platforms provided by regulatory authorities for submitting chemical usage and safety compliance reports.
Interaction: The Health and Safety Department generates reports using data from the tracking system and uploads them to government portals (e.g., for controlled substances like ephedrine).
Learning Management System (LMS) or Training Records Database
Purpose: Manages staff training and certifications.
Interaction: Health and Safety staff may need to verify whether a chemist is trained to handle hazardous chemicals by accessing records in this external system.
Purpose: Handles the logistics of chemical storage and retrieval at scale.
Interaction: In larger operations, a WMS may handle physical stock movement while the chemical tracking system focuses on chemical-level detail and regulatory data.
Purpose: Manages lab workflows, experiment tracking, and sample data.
Interaction: Lab staff might cross-reference chemicals used in experiments with the tracking system.
Purpose: Tracks spending, budgeting, and vendor payments.
Interaction: While not directly connected, reports from the tracking system may inform financial analysis or audits.
are offering similar functionalities and compare your idea with that project
List of all the documents/ projects that you have looked up as reference material for this project along with their links/references.
Purpose: Integrate core business processes, including procurement, finance, and inventory management.
Examples: SAP, Oracle NetSuite, Microsoft Dynamics.
Relevance: While ERPs manage overall business operations, they may lack specialized features for detailed chemical tracking and compliance.
Purpose: Manage laboratory workflows, sample tracking, and data management.
Relevance: LIMS are tailored for laboratory environments, offering functionalities like sample tracking and experiment data management.
Purpose: Oversee warehouse operations, including inventory tracking and order fulfillment.
Examples: Fishbowl, NetSuite WMS, Manhattan Associates.
Relevance: WMS focus on the physical movement and storage of goods but may not provide detailed chemical compliance features.
Purpose: Specifically designed to track chemical inventories, ensure safety compliance, and manage hazardous materials.
Relevance: These systems offer specialized features like Safety Data Sheet (SDS) management, regulatory reporting, and hazard classification.
: Feature comparison of our product with competitors

description establishes the boundary and connections between the system youâ€™re developing and everything else in the universe. The context diagram visually illustrates this boundary. It identifies external entities (also called terminators) outside the system that interface to it in some way, as well as data, control, and material flows between the terminators and the system. The context diagram is the top level in a data flow diagram developed according to the principles of structured analysis

illustrates a portion of the context diagram for the Chemical Tracking System. The entire system is depicted as a single circle; the context diagram deliberately provides no visibility into the systemâ€™s internal objects, processes, or data. The â€œsystemâ€ inside the circle could encompass any combination of software, hardware, and human components. Therefore, it could include manual operations as part of the entire system. The external entities in the rectangles can represent user classes (Chemist, Buyer), organizations (Health and Safety Department), other systems (Training Database), or hardware devices (Bar Code Reader). The arrows on the diagram represent the flow of data (such as a request for a chemical) or physical items (such as a chemical container) between the system and its external entities.
Identifies actual data flows and data stores
provide a big-picture view of how data moves through a system, which other models donâ€™t show well. Various people and systems execute processes that use, manipulate, and produce data
diagram canâ€™t show you the full life cycle of a piece of data.

ultiple pieces of data might be pulled together and transformed by a process (for example, shopping cart contents plus shipping information plus billing information are transformed into an order object).
the context diagram into a level 0 DFD which partitions the system into its major processes.
Figure shows a partial level 0 DFD for the Chemical Tracking System.

The single circle that represented the entire Chemical Tracking System on the context diagram has been subdivided into six major processes (the process bubbles). As with the context diagram, the external entities are shown in rectangles. All data flows (arrows) from the context diagram also appear on the level 0 DFD. In addition, the level 0 diagram contains several data stores, depicted as a pair of parallel horizontal lines,

which are internal to the system and therefore do not appear on the context diagram. A flow from a bubble to a store indicates that data is being placed into the store, a flow out of the store shows a read operation, and a bidirectional arrow between a store and a bubble indicates an update operation.
Each process that appears as a separate bubble on the level 0 diagram can be further expanded into a separate DFD to reveal more detail about its functioning
We are giving a sample list of Actors of online Learning platforms such as
our own developed by UMT named

all the actors must also be mentioned in your list of stakeholders
who creates a course one an online learning platform
who registers in a course and attempt, quizzes, assignments and exam and earn a certificate.
this person who is interested in online learning platform.
this person who represents a company. On company license multiple employees of the company can use on online learning platform.
view a site map so that all pages are indexed.
provide links to our web site and get paid for referring people to the site.
We are giving a sample Product backlog of online Learning platforms
or our own developed by UMT named

Online learning platforms are digital spaces where people can learn new skills, gain knowledge, and advance their education, all from the comfort of their own homes. Think of them as virtual schools or universities, but with a lot more flexibility and accessibility
Following are important concepts related to Product backlog:
: Epic vs User Story in Product Backlog

In Agile project management, a sprint is a fixed-length period during which a team works to complete a set of tasks and deliver a potentially shippable increment of product. Sprints are typically one to four weeks long, but two to three weeks is a common duration.
An epic is a user story that is too large to fully implement in a single iteration.

a multi-sprint project goal or a broad area of functionality that requires significant development effort. Because epics span iterations, they must be split into sets of smaller stories. Sometimes epics are large enough that they must be subdivided into multiple epics, each of which is then split into multiple stories until each resulting story can be reliably estimated and then implemented and tested within a single iteration. Breaking epics down into smaller epics and then into user stories is often referred to as story decomposition
Covers multiple releases or iterations and often spans across several teams.
Provides a high-level overview of the project and helps in prioritizing and planning the release.
A specific piece of functionality or a deliverable within an Epic that provides value to the user.
Smaller than an Epic and can be developed and released in a single iteration.
Breaks down the Epic into manageable chunks and guides the development process.
"Product listing page," "Order page," "Payment page" within the "e-commerce functionality" Epic.

A user story is a â€œshort, simple description of a feature told from the perspective of the person who desires the new capability, usually a user or customer of the systemâ€. User stories are sized so as to be fully implementable in a single iteration. Each completed user story iteratively builds the feature. User stories often are written according to the following template, although other styles also are used:
As a <type of user>, I want <some goal> so that <some reason OR Benefit>.
The smallest unit of work that can be delivered within a sprint.
Captures the requirements of a feature in a way that is easy for developers to understand and implement.
"As a user, I want to add items to my shopping cart so that I can buy them later"
A set of conditions or rules that a user story must satisfy to be considered "done" and accepted by the product owner.
To define what success looks like for a user story.
Usually written by the product owner, sometimes with input from QA and developers.
Often in plain language or BDD style (Given-When-Then).
User Story: As a user, I want to reset my password so I can access my account if I forget it.
The user must be able to request a password reset by entering their email.
The system must send a reset link if the email is registered.
The reset link must expire in 24 hours.
The user must be able to set a new password via the reset link.

In Agile, story points are a relative unit of measurement used to estimate the effort required to complete a user story or other piece of work. They are not time-based but rather represent the complexity and effort relative to other items on the backlog.
Key aspects of story points range in Agile:
Story points are assigned relative to other tasks, meaning a story with 2 points is roughly twice as complex as a story with 1 point.
Story points don't directly equate to hours or days; they represent a degree of effort or complexity.
Teams often use scales like Fibonacci (1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13...)
Story points help teams estimate work, plan sprints, and manage expectations, rather than focusing on individual time estimates.

It is a process where the product owner and development team collaboratively review and refine the product backlog. It is a crucial practice in Agile project management that involves regularly updating and refining the product backlog. It focuses on ensuring the backlog is well-defined, prioritized, and ready for upcoming sprint cycles, ultimately contributing to a more efficient and effective development process.
Refining user stories: Making sure they are clear, concise, and easy to understand.
Breaking down large items: Dividing complex tasks into smaller, more manageable units.
Estimating effort: Assessing how much work each item will require.
Prioritizing items: Determining which tasks should be tackled first.
Adding new items: Incorporating newly discovered requirements or features.
Removing outdated items: Eliminating items that are no longer relevant.
A well-groomed backlog allows the team to effectively plan and execute sprints.
The process promotes communication and collaboration between the product owner and development team.
By refining the backlog, the team can better predict the scope and effort required for upcoming sprints.
Ensures the backlog reflects the latest needs and priorities of the product.
By optimizing the backlog, the team can work more efficiently and deliver more value.
A well-defined backlog helps to minimize the risk of scope creep and delays.
When the team understands what they are working on and why, they are more motivated and engaged
Sub headings in this section are epics and each epic contain multiple user stories.
As an instructor, I can create a description of a course so that prospective participants can learn about it.
- Instructor can add/edit a course description.
- Course description is saved and displayed correctly.
As an instructor, I can maintain a
bio about myself so that it is used on any course listing for which Iâ€™m the instructor.
As a site visitor, I can view a detailed page about each course so that I can learn all about it.
- Course page displays course title, description, instructor bio, and related courses.
As a site visitor, I can browse a catalog of all courses available so that I can pick the right one for me.
- Course catalog lists all available courses.
- Filter and search functionality work correctly.
As a site visitor, I can see a list of other related courses when viewing a course so that I can see other courses I might be interested in.
- Related courses are displayed based on category, instructor, or tags.
As an instructor, I can create a description of a course so that
prospective participants can learn about it.
- Instructor can add/edit a course description.
description is saved and displayed correctly.
maintain a bio about myself so that it is used on any course listing for which Iâ€™m the instructor.
- Bio appears on all relevant course pages.
As a site visitor, I can view a detailed page about each course so that I can learn all about it.
- Course page displays course title, description, instructor bio, and related courses.
As a site visitor, I can browse a catalog of all courses available so that I can pick the right one for me.
- Course catalog lists all available courses.
- Filter and search functionality work correctly.
As a site visitor, I can see a list of other related courses when viewing a course so that I can see other courses I might be interested in.
- Related courses are displayed based on category, instructor, or tags.
login into the system and see
As an instructor, I can upload videos to be part of a course so that participants can watch those videos.
- Instructor can upload and organize videos.
- Videos play correctly on all devices.
As an instructor, I can create a quiz to include in a video course so that participants can take those quizzes to ensure they are learning.
- Instructor can add questions and answers.
- Participants can submit answers and get immediate feedback.
As an instructor, I can properly sequence the elements of a course so that I can assemble them into an amazing course.
- Instructor can arrange content in preferred order.
- Course displays in the correct sequence.
As an instructor, I can set the publication date for course elements so that items become
available when I want and not sooner.
- Content becomes accessible only on the
As a trainer, I can set the prices for my course so that I can make filthy lucre.
- Prices are correctly displayed on course pages.
As a site visitor, I can pay for the courses in my cart with a credit card so that I can purchase them and participate in (watch) them.
- User receives purchase confirmation and access.
As a site visitor, I can pay for the courses in my cart with PayPal so that I can purchase them and participate in (watch) them.
As a user, I can view the license terms before purchasing or subscribing so that I know what Iâ€™m getting.
- License terms are displayed before purchase.
- Users must accept terms before completing purchase.
can sign up for a monthly or annual subscription so that I can take all courses.
- Users can upgrade, downgrade, or cancel subscriptions.
As a company, I can purchase multiple licenses so that I can manage course registrations for a group of people.
- Admins can assign licenses to employees.
As a company, I manage a set of course registrations so that I can control which employees in my company have access to which courses.
- Company admin can manage access rights.
As a buyer, I can easily apply a license I buy to myself so that I can instantly start viewing a course.
As a buyer, I can distribute out licenses to others in my company so that they can view a course I pay for.
As a buyer, I can share a company-wide
license with everyone in my company so that everyone in the company can view the course.
As a participant, I want each course element marked as viewed after I view or complete it so that I can see which parts of a course Iâ€™ve completed.
As a participant, I can re-watch any video so that I can make sure I understand it.
As a site visitor, I can view some videos for free so that I can decide if I want to buy the full course.
- Users can play previews without logging in.
As a participant, I can set a mode on the videos so that one begins as the
previous one finishes so that I can watch without having to touch my keyboard, mouse or screen.
As a participant, I can earn a certificate of completion by finishing a course so that I have proof that I completed a course.
- Users can download or share certificates.
As a participant, I can earn a badge showing that I completed a course so that I can display that badge on my own website.
- Users can embed the badge externally.
As a site visitor, I can read an FAQ so that all my questions are answered.
- Common questions and answers are listed.
As a search engine, I can view a site map
so that all pages are indexed.
As a site visitor, I can sign up for a newsletter so that I get announcements about new courses and other information.
As a participant, I want the site to save my progress in a quiz or video in case I lose connection so that I donâ€™t have to start over.
- Progress is saved every few minutes.
- Users can resume quizzes or videos from where they left off.
As a site visitor, I can access a help center with guides and FAQs so that I can quickly resolve common issues.
- Help center includes guides, FAQs, and contact support.
- Users can search for help topics.
As a participant, I can participate in a forum for a course so that I can discuss questions and topics with the instructor and other participants.
- Participants and instructors can post and reply.
As an instructor, I can post announcements to a project 'home page' for a Scheduled Course so that I can tell participants about important things such as 'Iâ€™m delayed in posting chapter 7.'
- Instructors can create and edit announcements.
As a participant, I can read announcements from an instructor for a Scheduled Course so that I can keep up to date on news.
- Announcements are clearly visible on the course home page.
As an instructor, I can create quizzes so that participants can assess their learning.
- Participants can take quizzes and get scores.
As a participant, I can take a quiz as part of a course so that I can get feedback on how well I learned
As a participant, I can navigate a quiz by submitting an answer or by pressing skip (forward) or back so that I can answer questions in a different order if Iâ€™d like.
- Users can navigate back and forth in quizzes.
As a participant, I can get answers via video rather than text so that I watch rather than read an answer.
- Video explanations are available for quiz answers.
- Users can choose between text or video explanations.
As a participant, I want quiz responses stored in the database so that I can see how people are doing at various questions, perhaps so I can fix one that is worded poorly.
- Quiz responses are stored and retrievable.
As a participant, I can navigate a quiz by submitting an answer or by pressing skip (forward) or
back so that I can answer questions in a different order if Iâ€™d like.
- Users can skip or go back to previous questions.
As a visitor, I want to be able to view the site and videos in any reasonable browser so that I can use what Iâ€™m accustomed to.
- The site is compatible with major browsers.
As a partner company, I would like to provide links to
and get paid for referring people to the site so that I can become part of the 1%.
As a partner company, I can embed
(free ones only) on my site so that people can enjoy those videos on my site
possibly giving me affiliate revenue but possibly just being about
putting good content on my site.
- Free content is available for sharing.
As a participant, I can leave a comment about a course so that I can tell the world what I think of it.
- Comments are moderated and displayed correctly.
As an instructor, I can leave a comment about a sample video or the course so that I can respond to other comments.
- Comments are visible and properly threaded.
As a participant, I am shown an evaluation form
after completing a course so that I can tell
what I think of their classes and their trainers.
- Users can submit feedback and ratings.
As an instructor, I can view all feedback for a course when logged in so that I can see historical or aggregate data on my courses.
- Data is aggregated for trend analysis.
As an instructor, I can opt into receiving an email of every course evaluation as it is completed by a participant so that I get real-time feedback on my courses.
- Instructors receive email notifications of evaluations.
As an instructor, I can export all feedback for a course to a CSV file so that I can perform more in-depth analysis.
- CSV file contains all evaluation data.
As a site owner, I receive a periodic email summarizing registrations for the day, week, and month to date so that I know how
As a trainer, I receive a periodic sales summary at the frequencies I select so that I know how my courses are doing.
As a trainer, I can generate reports on participant progress so that I can monitor their learning outcomes.
- Reports include participant scores and completion status.
- Trainers can filter reports by date or course.
As a sys admin, I can run any report a trainer can run so that I can see overall information for any trainer on the site.
- Data is exportable and properly formatted.

repo where they would be maintaining their code Baselines.
The goal of the first Sprint is to implement core functionalities including user authentication, dashboard, and profile management.
Sprint 1 â€“ Initial Setup & Core Architecture
Facilitated by: FYP Supervisor. Product Owner
Backlog Items with high priority, high risk and Architecturaly Significant
As an instructor, I can create a description of a course so that prospective participants can learn about
- Instructor can add/edit a course description.
- Course description is saved and displayed
As an instructor, I can sign, login and maintain a bio about myself so that it is used on any course listing for which Iâ€™m the instructor.
- Bio appears on all relevant course pages.
As a site visitor, I can view a detailed page about each course so that I can learn all about it.
- Course page displays course title, description, instructor bio, and related courses.
As a site visitor, I can browse a catalog of all courses available so that I can pick the right one for me.
- Course catalog lists all available courses.
- Filter and search functionality work correctly.
As a site visitor, I can see a list of other related courses when viewing a course so that I can see other courses I might be interested in.
- Related courses are displayed based on category, instructor, or tags.
As a student, I can Sign up, login into the system and see my registered courses (if

itself further hands off the calculation to the Product
note how we show the passing of a parameter. Similarly, to calculate the discount,
from the Order to do this, the Customer makes a reentrant call (getBaseVal ue)
to the Order to get the data.
A software system is often governed by complex logic, with various combinations of conditions leading to different system behaviors.

In such kind of user stories, it is advisable to create decision table in order to better understand possible combinations. Tesers are also advised to develop a separate test case for each possible combinations that check whether system is working fine in all those complex possible combinationss of input and outputs.
Figure below shows a decision table for the logic that governs whether the Chemical Tracking System should accept or reject each request for a new chemical. Four factors influence this decision:
Whether the user who is creating the request is authorized to request chemicals
Whether the chemical is available either in the chemical stockroom or from a vendor
Whether the chemical is on the list of hazardous chemicals that require special training in safe handling
Whether the user who is creating the request has been trained in handling this type of hazardous chemical.
Test cases should be made against each user story to test the functionality.
: Verify Account Creation with Valid Data
is easily able to register an account with correct details.

email address field is being filled appropriately.

Registration form could use better validation
DB schema is clean and extendable

Daily standups were effective and focused

More thorough test coverage for backend APIs
Add Swagger API docs to backend
Team to move into Sprint 2 focused on Course Management features.
Sprint: Sprint 1 â€“ Initial Setup & Core Architecture
Establish the foundational architecture of the MOOC platform and implement user authentication module.
Successful implementation of authentication with JWT
CI/CD pipeline setup early and was stable
Team was responsive and collaborative during standups
Clean database schema laid a strong foundation
UI validation rules were inconsistent across components
Test coverage was minimal due to time constraints
Ambiguity in acceptance criteria for some stories
Create a shared UI component style guide
Include test case planning in story grooming sessions
Improve story definitions and add clearer acceptance criteria
Use pair programming for complex backend logic
Add test planning checklist to grooming doc
Refine story templates with clearer definitions
Sufficient but can improve in UI design guidance
Team expressed overall satisfaction with the sprint outcomes and is aligned on areas of improvement. Looking forward to Sprint 2.
Describe the system architecture, or simply provide the architecture diagram

A System Context diagram is a good starting point for diagramming and documenting a software system, allowing you to step back and see the big picture. Draw a diagram showing your system as a box in the
, surrounded by its users and the other systems that it interacts with.
Detail isnâ€™t important here as this is your zoomed out view showing a big picture of the system landscape. The focus should be on people (actors, roles, personas,
) and software systems rather than technologies, protocols and other low-level details. Itâ€™s the sort of diagram that you could show to non-technical people.

Once you understand how your system fits in to the overall IT environment, a really useful next step is to zoom-in to the system boundary with a Container diagram. A â€œcontainerâ€ is something like a server-side web application, single-page application, desktop application, mobile app, database schema, file system, etc. Essentially, a container is a separately runnable/deployable unit (
a separate process space) that executes code or stores data.

The Container diagram shows the high-level shape of the software architecture and how responsibilities are distributed across it. It also shows the major technology choices and how the containers communicate with one another. Itâ€™s a simple, high-level technology
diagram that is useful for software developers and support/operations staff alike.
Next you can zoom in and decompose each container further to identify the major structural building blocks and their interactions.
The Component diagram shows how a container is made up of a number of â€œcomponentsâ€, what each of those components are, their responsibilities and the technology/implementation details.
Finally, you can zoom in to each component to show how it is implemented as code; using UML class diagrams, entity relationship diagrams or similar.
This is an optional level of detail and is often available on-demand from tooling such as IDEs. Ideally this diagram would be automatically generated using tooling (

an IDE or UML modelling tool), and you should consider showing only those attributes and methods that allow you to tell the story that you want to tell. This level of detail is not recommended for anything but the most important or complex components.
We now present another example, a UNIVERSITY database,

to illustrate the ER modeling concepts. Suppose that a database is needed to keep track of student enrollments in classes and studentsâ€™ final grades. After analyzing the miniworld rules and the usersâ€™ needs, the requirements for this database were determined to be as follows (for brevity, we show the chosen entity type names and attribute names for the conceptual schema in parentheses as we describe the requirements; relationship type names are only shown in the ER schema diagram):

The university is organized into colleges (COLLEGE), and each college has a unique name (CName), a main office (COffice) and phone (CPhone), and a particular faculty member who is dean of the college. Each college administers a number of academic departments (DEPT). Each department has a unique name (DName), a unique code number (DCode), a main office (DOffice) and phone (DPhone), and a particular faculty member who chairs the department. We keep track of the start date (CStartDate) when that faculty member began chairing the department.

A department offers a number of courses (COURSE), each of which has a unique course name (CoName), a unique code number (CCode), a course level (Level: this can be coded as 1 for freshman level, 2 for sophomore, 3 for junior, 4 for senior, 5 for MS level, and 6 for PhD level), a course credit hours (Credits), and a course description (CDesc). The database also keeps track of instructors (INSTRUCTOR); and each instructor has a unique identifier (Id), name (IName), office (IOffice), phone (IPhone), and rank (Rank);
in addition, each instructor works for one primary academic department.

The database will keep student data (STUDENT) and stores each studentâ€™s name (SName, composed of first name (FName), middle name (MName), last name (LName)), student id (Sid, unique for every student), address (Addr), phone (Phone), major code (Major), and date of birth (DoB). A student is assigned to one primary academic department. It is required to keep track of the studentâ€™s grades in each section the student has completed.

Courses are offered as sections (SECTION). Each section is related to a single course and a single instructor and has a unique section identifier (SecId). A section also has a section number (SecNo: this is coded as 1, 2, 3, . . . for multiple sections offered during the same semester/year), semester (Sem), year (Year), classroom (CRoom: this is coded as a combination of building code (Bldg) and room number (RoomNo) within the building), and days/times (DaysTime: for example, â€˜MWF 9am-9.50amâ€™ or â€˜TR 3.30pm-5.20pmâ€™â€” restricted to only allowed days/time values). (Note: The database will keep track of all the sections offered for the past several years, in addition to the current offerings. The SecId is unique for all sections, not just the sections for a particular semester.) The database keeps track of the students in each section, and the grade is recorded when available (this is a many-to-many relationship between students and sections). A section must have at least five students.
The ER diagram for these requirements is shown in Figure 28 using the min-max ER diagrammatic notation. Notice that for the SECTION entity type, we only showed
as an underlined key, but because of the
constraints, several other combinations of values have to be unique for each section entity. For example, each of the following combinations must be unique based on the typical
(of the COURSE related to the SECTION)): This specifies that the section numbers of a particular course must be different during each particular semester and year.
): This specifies that in a particular semester and year, a classroom cannot be used by two different sections at the same days/time.

, Id (of the INSTRUCTOR teaching the SECTION)): This specifies that in a particular semester and year, an instructor cannot teach two sections at the same days/time. Note that this rule will not apply if an instructor is allowed to teach two combined sections together in the particular university

is a collection of detailed information about the data entities used in an application. Collecting the information about composition, data types, allowed values, and the like into a shared resource identifies the data validation criteria, helps developers write programs correctly, and minimizes integration problems. The data dictionary complements the project glossary, which defines application domain or business terms, abbreviations, and acronyms. We recommend keeping the data dictionary and the glossary separate.

This information feeds into design in the form of database schemas, tables, and attributes, which ultimately lead to variable names in programs. The time you invest in creating a data dictionary will be more than repaid by avoiding the mistakes that can result when project participants have different understandings of the data.
sample data dictionary of chemical tracking system.
: Data Dictionary of Chemical Tracking System

How and where was your software deployed? Did you face any problems, how did you overcome these
Entire code of software is not required. Just highlight your important (user defined/ improved) algorithms.

, Select and Mellon Bank to help design the interfaces between their systems and

There will be no real-time interfacing with any accounting systems.
AÂ constraintÂ specifies how theÂ systemÂ must operate or how it must be built
Constraints applied on the system by the client
Services your software is unable to provide
Use the matrix & values from 7.3.1 to show that all requirements are being fulfilled.
Use the matrix & values from 7.3.3 to show that all requirements have been implemented correctly.
Use the matrix & values from 7.3.4 to show that all requirements have been tested to be conforming to requirements.
Use IEEE or ACM format for citations
and Joy Beatty. 2013. Software Requirements 3. Microsoft Press, USA.
. 2001. Applying UML and Patterns: An Introduction to Object-Oriented Analysis and Design and the Unified Process (2nd. ed.). Prentice Hall PTR, USA.
. 2015. Fundamentals of Database Systems (7th. ed.). Pearson.
Martin Fowler. 2003. UML Distilled: A Brief Guide to the Standard Object Modeling Language (3rd. ed.). Addison-Wesley Longman Publishing Co., Inc., USA.
Must use contents of development/ deployment setup & external system dependencies
